prompt: All hardware accelerators used in the datacenter context use seamless programming interfaces and software frameworks, and can be easily interchanged.

subStatements.statement: All hardware accelerators used in the datacenter context use seamless programming interfaces and software frameworks, and can be easily interchanged.
subStatements.motivation: From the Server / hardware-accelerators material, GPUs, TPUs and FPGAs are heterogeneous, domain-specific devices, each with its own programming model and software stack (e.g. CUDA for NVIDIA GPUs, XLA/TensorFlow for TPUs, HDL/HLS toolchains for FPGAs). They are NOT interchangeable and there is no single seamless interface common to all of them. The universal claim is false.

solution: See per-statement motivation. Accelerators are heterogeneous with vendor/device-specific stacks and are not freely interchangeable - the statement is **False**.

prompt: Warehouse-scale computers are single servers that are designed to be used in large-scale data centers.

subStatements.statement: Warehouse-scale computers are single servers that are designed to be used in large-scale data centers.
subStatements.motivation: The Data Center / WSC lecture defines a Warehouse-Scale Computer as a whole data center treated as a *single, massive computer*: thousands of commodity servers, storage and networking running a common, homogeneous workload at warehouse scale. It is the opposite of 'a single server'. The statement is false.

solution: See per-statement motivation. A WSC is an entire datacenter acting as one computer, not a single server - the statement is **False**.

prompt: Docker and Kubernetes are two technology examples supporting para-virtualization.

subStatements.statement: Docker and Kubernetes are two technology examples supporting para-virtualization.
subStatements.motivation: From the Virtualization lecture, para-virtualization is a hypervisor-level technique where a modified guest OS cooperates with the hypervisor (e.g. Xen). Docker and Kubernetes are **container** technologies: Docker provides OS-level virtualization (containers share the host kernel) and Kubernetes is a container orchestrator. They are not examples of para-virtualization. The statement is false.

solution: See per-statement motivation. Docker/Kubernetes are container (OS-level virtualization / orchestration) technologies, not para-virtualization - the statement is **False**.

prompt: East-west traffic refers to network traffic that travels between servers within the same datacenter.

subStatements.statement: East-west traffic refers to network traffic that travels between servers within the same datacenter.
subStatements.motivation: The Datacenter Networking lecture distinguishes **north-south** traffic (between the datacenter and the outside world / Internet) from **east-west** traffic (server-to-server communication inside the datacenter). Modern distributed and AI workloads make east-west traffic dominant. So the statement is true.

solution: See per-statement motivation. East-west = intra-datacenter server-to-server traffic - the statement is **True**.

prompt: The average seek time of an HDD can be estimated as one-third of the time it takes the actuator arm to move from the innermost to the outermost track.

subStatements.statement: The average seek time of an HDD can be estimated as one-third of the time it takes the actuator arm to move from the innermost to the outermost track.
subStatements.motivation: From the HDD lecture, the seek time depends on the distance the head travels. Averaging over all possible start/end track pairs uniformly distributed over the full stroke, the expected seek distance is about one third of the full-stroke distance, so the **average seek time is estimated as ~1/3 of the full-stroke (innermost-to-outermost) seek time**. The statement is true.

solution: See per-statement motivation. The average seek is approximately 1/3 of the full-stroke seek time - the statement is **True**.

prompt: Replicating data across availability zones helps ensure data durability in case of local failures.

subStatements.statement: Replicating data across availability zones helps ensure data durability in case of local failures.
subStatements.motivation: Availability Zones are isolated failure domains (separate power, cooling, networking) within a region. By replicating data across multiple AZs, a local failure (power/hardware/fire in one zone) does not destroy the only copy, so the data survives and remains durable. This is exactly the rationale for cross-AZ replication. The statement is true.

solution: See per-statement motivation. Cross-AZ replication protects data against single-location failures, improving durability - the statement is **True**.

prompt: 3-tier datacenter networks consist of three distinct layers named core, aggregation, and access.

subStatements.statement: 3-tier datacenter networks consist of three distinct layers named core, aggregation, and access.
subStatements.motivation: The Datacenter Networking lecture describes the classical three-tier (three-layer) switch-centric architecture as exactly three layers: **Core**, **Aggregation** (distribution), and **Access** (edge/ToR). So the naming in the statement matches the course material - it is true.

solution: See per-statement motivation. The classical 3-tier DC network is Core / Aggregation / Access - the statement is **True**.

prompt: Data centers using HDDs typically ignore redundancy features, assuming disk failures are very rare.

subStatements.statement: Data centers using HDDs typically ignore redundancy features, assuming disk failures are very rare.
subStatements.motivation: Disk failures are NOT rare at datacenter scale: with thousands of HDDs, drive failures are a routine, expected event. This is precisely why data centers rely heavily on **redundancy** - RAID, replication and erasure coding - to tolerate disk failures without data loss. Ignoring redundancy would guarantee data loss. The statement is false.

solution: See per-statement motivation. At scale HDD failures are common, so datacenters use RAID/replication redundancy - the statement is **False**.

prompt: Cloud-native applications are designed to run in the cloud and often use microservices and containers.

subStatements.statement: Cloud-native applications are designed to run in the cloud and often use microservices and containers.
subStatements.motivation: The Cloud Computing lecture characterises cloud-native applications as built specifically for cloud platforms, decomposed into loosely-coupled **microservices**, packaged in **containers** and orchestrated (e.g. Kubernetes) for elasticity and scalability. This matches the statement exactly - it is true.

solution: See per-statement motivation. Cloud-native = built for the cloud using microservices and containers - the statement is **True**.

prompt: Open-loop cooling exploits favourable outdoor conditions to reduce mechanical cooling load.

subStatements.statement: Open-loop cooling exploits favourable outdoor conditions to reduce mechanical cooling load.
subStatements.motivation: In the Building Infrastructures lecture, open-loop cooling is **fresh-air / free cooling**: it draws in cool outside air to remove heat directly, instead of running power-hungry mechanical chillers/compressors. When the outdoor air is cool and dry it substantially reduces (or removes) the mechanical cooling load and improves PUE. So the statement is true.

solution: See per-statement motivation. Open-loop (free/fresh-air) cooling uses favourable outdoor air to cut mechanical cooling load - the statement is **True**.

prompt: Using a two-tier leaf-spine topology without oversubscription and adopting only switches with **8 ports** (all switches have the same number of ports), what is the maximum number of servers that can be connected?
solution:
**Method (homogeneous leaf-spine / Clos design).** With all switches having the same number of ports $2k$, the lecture's homogeneous leaf-spine result is:
- each **leaf** switch uses $k$ ports downward (to servers) and $k$ ports upward (to spines),
- there are $k$ spine switches and $t = 2k$ leaf switches,
- maximum number of servers $= 2k^2$.

Here each switch has $2k = 8$ ports $\Rightarrow k = 4$.

$$N_{servers} = 2k^2 = 2 \times 4^2 = 2 \times 16 = 32$$

(Check: $k=4$ ports per leaf go to servers, $k=4$ up to the $k=4$ spines, $t=2k=8$ leaf switches $\Rightarrow 8 \times 4 = 32$ servers, non-blocking.)

$$\boxed{N_{servers} = 32\ \text{servers}}$$

prompt: A critical service relies on three components. **A** is a central control unit, mandatory for operation (if A fails the service is unavailable). **B1** and **B2** are redundant processing units, only one of which needs to work. The reliability of A after 2 years is $R_A(2\,\text{y})=0.8$ and its $MTTR=14$ days. The availabilities of B1 and B2 are $Av_{B1}=0.75$ and $Av_{B2}=0.85$. Compute the availability of component A and the overall system availability.
solution:
**Method.** A is in **series** with the parallel block B1||B2 (1-of-2). For A, recover its $MTTF$ from the exponential reliability $R(t)=e^{-t/MTTF}$, then use $Av = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF+MTTR}$.

**1) MTTF of A** (with $t=2$ years):
$$R_A(2)=e^{-2/MTTF_A}=0.8 \;\Rightarrow\; MTTF_A=\frac{-2}{\ln 0.8}=\frac{2}{0.22314}=8.963\ \text{years}$$

**2) Availability of A** (convert $MTTR=14$ days $=14/365=0.03836$ y):
$$Av_A=\frac{MTTF_A}{MTTF_A+MTTR}=\frac{8.963}{8.963+0.03836}=\frac{8.963}{9.0017}=0.99574$$

**3) Parallel block B1||B2** (works if at least one works):
$$Av_B = 1-(1-Av_{B1})(1-Av_{B2}) = 1-(0.25)(0.15)=1-0.0375=0.9625$$

**4) Overall system** (A in series with B):
$$Av_{sys}=Av_A \cdot Av_B = 0.99574 \times 0.9625$$
$$\boxed{Av_A \approx 0.9957,\qquad Av_{sys} \approx 0.9584\ (\approx 95.84\%)}$$

prompt: Design a storage system using a **RAID 1+0** configuration of HDDs with capacity 24 GB each. Each disk has $MTTF=420$ days and $MTTR=4$ days. What is the **maximum number of disks** that can be included while ensuring the overall storage $MTTF_{RAID1+0}$ is at least **8 years**?
solution:
**Method.** For RAID 1+0 with a single mirror replica, the exercise sessions use
$$MTTF_{RAID1+0}=\frac{MTTF^2}{N\cdot MTTR}$$
where $N$ is the total number of disks. Impose $MTTF_{RAID1+0}\ge 8$ years.

Convert the target: $8\ \text{y}=8\times 365 = 2920\ \text{days}$.

$$\frac{MTTF^2}{N\cdot MTTR}\ge 2920 \;\Rightarrow\; N\le \frac{MTTF^2}{MTTR\cdot 2920}=\frac{420^2}{4\times 2920}=\frac{176400}{11680}=15.10$$

So $N\le 15.10$. Because RAID 1+0 mirrors disks in pairs, $N$ must be **even**, so the largest admissible value is:
$$\boxed{N_{max}=14\ \text{disks}}$$

(With $N=14$: $MTTF_{RAID1+0}=420^2/(14\times4)=176400/56=3150$ days $\approx 8.6$ y $\ge 8$ y. With $N=16$ it would drop to $\approx 7.5$ y, below the requirement.)

prompt: An Internet-based system has a Web server, an application server and a DBMS. Monitoring for $T=1800$ s gives: completions $C=600$, $U_{Web}=75\%$, $U_{App}=50\%$, $U_{DBMS}=60\%$. What are the service demands of the three servers ($D_{Web}$, $D_{App}$, $D_{DBMS}$)?
solution:
**Method (operational laws).** System throughput $X=C/T$; service demand from the utilization law $U_k=X\,D_k \Rightarrow D_k=U_k/X$.

**System throughput:**
$$X=\frac{C}{T}=\frac{600}{1800}=0.3333\ \text{req/s}$$

**Service demands** $D_k=U_k/X$:
$$D_{Web}=\frac{0.75}{0.3333}=2.25\ \text{s}$$
$$D_{App}=\frac{0.50}{0.3333}=1.50\ \text{s}$$
$$D_{DBMS}=\frac{0.60}{0.3333}=1.80\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D_{Web}=2.25\ \text{s},\quad D_{App}=1.50\ \text{s},\quad D_{DBMS}=1.80\ \text{s}}$$

prompt: For the system of Question 14 ($D_{Web}=2.25$ s, $D_{App}=1.50$ s, $D_{DBMS}=1.80$ s), the incoming load is expected to grow to $\lambda=5\ \text{req/s}$. Determine the **minimum number of servers at each physical layer** so that the utilization of each server is at most **55%** (servers of the same type evenly distribute their load).
solution:
**Method.** With $n_k$ identical servers at layer $k$ sharing the load evenly, the per-server utilization at arrival rate $\lambda$ (in equilibrium $X=\lambda$) is $U_{server}=\dfrac{\lambda\,D_k}{n_k}$. Impose $U_{server}\le 0.55$:
$$n_k \ge \frac{\lambda\,D_k}{0.55}$$

**Web** ($D=2.25$): $\dfrac{5\times 2.25}{0.55}=\dfrac{11.25}{0.55}=20.45 \Rightarrow n_{Web}=21$

**App** ($D=1.50$): $\dfrac{5\times 1.50}{0.55}=\dfrac{7.5}{0.55}=13.64 \Rightarrow n_{App}=14$

**DBMS** ($D=1.80$): $\dfrac{5\times 1.80}{0.55}=\dfrac{9.0}{0.55}=16.36 \Rightarrow n_{DBMS}=17$

$$\boxed{n_{Web}=21,\quad n_{App}=14,\quad n_{DBMS}=17}$$

prompt: Assume you upgraded the system according to your answer to Question 15 ($n_{Web}=21$, $n_{App}=14$, $n_{DBMS}=17$ with $D_{Web}=2.25$ s, $D_{App}=1.50$ s, $D_{DBMS}=1.80$ s). What is the **maximum throughput** the new system can achieve?
solution:
**Method.** Replicating a layer $n_k$ times divides its per-server demand to $D_k/n_k$. The bottleneck is the layer with the largest effective per-server demand, and $X_{max}=1/D_{max}^{eff}$ with $D_{max}^{eff}=\max_k (D_k/n_k)$.

**Effective per-server demands:**
$$\frac{D_{Web}}{n_{Web}}=\frac{2.25}{21}=0.10714\ \text{s}$$
$$\frac{D_{App}}{n_{App}}=\frac{1.50}{14}=0.10714\ \text{s}$$
$$\frac{D_{DBMS}}{n_{DBMS}}=\frac{1.80}{17}=0.10588\ \text{s}$$

$D_{max}^{eff}=0.10714$ s (Web and App tie as bottleneck).

$$X_{max}=\frac{1}{D_{max}^{eff}}=\frac{1}{0.10714}$$
$$\boxed{X_{max}\approx 9.33\ \text{req/s}}$$

The upgraded system comfortably sustains the target $\lambda=5$ req/s.

prompt: How does the **geographical distribution** of datacenters affect aspects such as **latency, reliability, and environmental sustainability**?
solution:
Geographically distributing datacenters across **regions** (each containing isolated **availability zones**) trades off three key concerns:

**1. Latency.** Network latency is bounded by physical distance (speed of light) plus the number of hops. Placing datacenters **close to end users** (and using edge locations / CDNs) reduces round-trip time, improving interactive and real-time application performance. A single central datacenter would serve far-away users with high, uneven latency; a geographically spread footprint lets each user be served from a nearby region.

**2. Reliability / availability.** Spreading capacity across multiple **regions** and **availability zones** creates independent failure domains. A localized disaster (power outage, fire, flood, earthquake, network cut) takes down only one site, while replicated services and data in other regions keep running - this is the basis of disaster recovery and geo-redundancy. More geographic diversity therefore increases overall reliability, durability (cross-region replication) and the ability to meet high-availability SLAs. The cost is the complexity of keeping replicas consistent and the extra WAN latency for synchronous replication.

**3. Environmental sustainability.** Location strongly affects the energy/carbon profile. Building datacenters where the **climate is cool** enables free/fresh-air or water-side economizer cooling, lowering the mechanical cooling load and improving **PUE**. Siting near **renewable or low-carbon energy** (hydro, wind, solar, geothermal) and near abundant water/grid capacity reduces carbon footprint and operating cost. Distributing load also lets operators follow cheap/clean energy and reduce strain on any single grid.

**Summary / trade-off.** Geographic distribution improves user-perceived latency and system reliability and enables greener siting (cool climates, renewable energy), but it raises cost and consistency/coordination complexity (WAN latency, data-replication overhead, regulatory/data-residency constraints). Good design picks regions to balance proximity to users, independence of failure domains, and access to cheap, low-carbon energy and efficient cooling.

prompt: Compare the main storage technologies used in datacenters - **HDDs, SSDs, and tapes**. In which scenarios is each one preferable? Discuss advantages, disadvantages, and selection criteria in terms of performance, cost, capacity, and reliability.
solution:
The three technologies sit at different points of the **performance / cost / capacity** trade-off, forming a storage hierarchy.

**HDD (Hard Disk Drive) - magnetic, mechanical.**
- *Advantages:* low cost per GB, high capacity, mature and reliable for bulk storage; good sequential throughput.
- *Disadvantages:* mechanical seek + rotational latency make **random access slow** (ms-scale); moving parts wear out and consume power; limited IOPS.
- *Best for:* large-capacity, cost-sensitive, mostly-sequential or warm data - bulk storage, backups, nearline data, large datasets where capacity-per-dollar matters more than latency.

**SSD (Solid-State Drive) - flash, no moving parts.**
- *Advantages:* very high **IOPS** and very low latency (microseconds), high throughput, low power, shock-resistant; excellent for random access.
- *Disadvantages:* higher cost per GB than HDD; finite **write endurance / wear** (limited program-erase cycles, need wear-leveling); historically lower capacity per device.
- *Best for:* hot data, latency-sensitive and random-I/O workloads - databases, OLTP, caching tiers, VM images, metadata, boot/scratch volumes.

**Tape - magnetic sequential media.**
- *Advantages:* the **lowest cost per GB**, enormous capacity, very long shelf life, offline (air-gapped) so resilient to ransomware/online failures, low idle energy.
- *Disadvantages:* purely **sequential access** with very high access latency (mount + seek to position); not suitable for any interactive/random access.
- *Best for:* cold/archival data, long-term backup and compliance retention - 'write once, rarely read' data.

**Selection criteria.**
- *Performance (latency/IOPS):* SSD > HDD > Tape.
- *Cost per GB:* Tape < HDD < SSD.
- *Capacity/density:* Tape and HDD are highest for bulk; SSDs are catching up but cost more.
- *Reliability/durability:* all need redundancy (RAID/replication/erasure coding); tape's offline nature gives strong protection for archives, SSDs avoid mechanical failures but have wear limits, HDDs have mechanical failure modes.

**Conclusion (tiering).** A datacenter combines all three: **SSD** for the hot, latency-critical tier; **HDD** for the warm, capacity-oriented tier; **tape** for the cold archival tier - placing each dataset on the medium whose cost/performance matches its access pattern.
